sentences.text: My name is Michla Turkienicz.
That's what I was called at home.
Yeah, that's my name, maiden name.
My name now is Snow, S-N-O-W.
I was born in Serniki.
It's a small town, S-E-R-N-I-K.
It's close to Pinsk, about two hours by boat.
We used to go there.
I was born the 25th of January, 1924.
My father's name was Markel.
And my mother's was Chana.
Yeah.
I still, thank God, have them, my sister, Itke, and my brother, Milton or Motle, how he was called at home.
Well, my town wasn't a big town.
But I-- I remember happy times in my town.
We were but 1,000 Jews.
And mostly, they lived in five streets.
And around, there were Christian people, there were farm.
And people were on good terms with them.
Of course, there were some bad people.
But I wouldn't say that all the-- the people were bad.
We had a store.
A store, a leather store.
Those who made the shoes used to come to us and buy their material.
My father was a gentle person, very educated.
He was a biblical scholar.
But like I said, he helped out when the rabbi had a hard case.
He used to come and-- and talk with him, how to-- how to handle it.
And always-- always were people in our house asking questions.
There were even marital problems.
When they used to come, he used to talk to them.
And they-- they somehow stayed together.
And there were people who had relatives in the United States.
And they used to come and ask him, he should write a letter.
Maybe they'll help them.
It was honest letter, because he wouldn't make up things.
He also belonged to-- it was like a committee or whatever from Jewish people.
They helped the poor.
It wasn't like here, that you have welfare or other things.
You always looked that everybody who comes to town should have a place to sleepover, to have a meal to eat.
He used to bring to the house quite often, even our house wasn't as big.
But quite often, they had some visitors.
And when the yeshiva bochurim came to our town, there was always-- he used to place them in houses.
They should be fed.
And he was a gentle, a very gentle, very, very quiet person but very educated.
Here, there used to come newspapers to our house.
I think 'The Moment' and 'The [INAUDIBLE].' It's not just that he knew the religious things.
He liked to-- to know what's going on in the world.
But he was a religious man.
He never wore the long clothes or with-- he used to have a hat.
Never-- a razor never went on his face.
But he didn't have a beard.
Yeah.
He was a friend.
He was, you know-- in a small town, usually, the educated, they-- they come together.
They discuss things.
The rabbi was Millner, his second name.
He had four sons, and all four were rabbis.
So one was-- they were all killed.
Just one, they ran away with the [NON-ENGLISH], and he lived in New York.
But the whole family is gone.
Well, my mother was from a different town.
But as I remember her-- I was only 10 years old when she died.
She was elegant, very elegant.
And she had, I think, a diploma from a high school, which, in those days, it wasn't like now.
And she married my father.
And when she-- when I was 10, my sister was 8, my brother was 6, she-- she-- she got sick.
She had cancer.
And she died.
She was sick two years.
And we were-- we were alone.
But my father had two sisters, who helped a lot.
They used to come in the morning and cook for us.
And there was a local girl, who cleaned.
And we managed, somehow.
We tried to cover, eat less.
I used to come from school.
I knew that I have to wash the dishes, so I have to do things for my brother and sister, my father.
He never remarried.
And we were maybe five years until the war started.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah, very difficult.
I used to think-- I used to be envy of my girlfriends that they-- they have parents.
I mean, we had a very good father and aunties, but that doesn't-- doesn't mean that.
I remember her funeral.
It was very cold.
And being young children, they didn't take us.
And there were a lot of crying, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's how it was.
Everybody had, like you say in Hebrew, [HEBREW] because we were orphans.
But I-- I-- I didn't like that somebody should have pity with me, with us.

Because I don't know why I kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and being independent probably helped me a lot, through the years to come, not to lean on anybody, rather give advice somebody, how to survive or other things.
Well, it was a Polish school.
And it was a Hebrew school.
In Polish school, I went just till seven grades, because, in my time, it wasn't a high school.
But Hebrew, after school, I went to Hebrew school.
And being my father's daughter, I had to learn.
So after-- so I-- I learned probably till the war started, a private teacher.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I liked.
I even spoke Hebrew.
Now, after so many years, I can understand.
But to speak it a little bit--
Yeah.
Yeah, they were mixed.
Not-- not when I was.
No, not when-- when I was.
There weren't too many Christian kids, because a lot of them they kept back.
They didn't send to school.
You have to-- they used to get-- that they have to pay money if they keep the kids out on the farms.
But not too many.
Well, it was, I think, a happy childhood.
Because we-- we belonged to our-- to Betar.
We used to come together a couple of times a week and dream about Israel.
As-- as my father was teaching us or in the Hebrew that Moshiach should come.
But they had other ideas how to reach those shores.
A lot of people, from my town, they went to [NON-ENGLISH] to go to Israel.
But I was too young to dream of it.
And we had some relatives in the United States.
But the-- the quarter was very closed.
So there was nowhere to go.
But we had like a dance, plays.
We used to go every Saturday night or Sunday and dance and read books and talk about books.
And like-- like any other small town in this.
I don't know.
I can't remember, really.
Well, most, to get married and have children and probably be good to each other.
As I say in my house, it was always to help somebody, that was the biggest achievement.
And by that time, if you-- if you marry, you have children.
And that was life.
Yeah.
One was Minuske.
Scared And one was Oske.
I had my mother's sister, there, Henia.
She had five daughters and a son.
And they all perished.
We were a very close family with all of them.
So that's-- it's not like-- I remember something.
One helped each other, maybe because nobody had nothing.
So helping was a big thing.
Yeah.
Well, it was always like, if somebody need help, that was his specialty.

But there were once, one of the ministers, of-- in Poland, said that when you kill a cow, in the Jewish way, they suffer more than just to-- And they wanted to-- to take it away from the Jewish people.
And most of them were religious.
So every town took a committee and went to the authority to ask for, they shouldn't do it.
So my father was picked, him.
And I think the rabbi's son.
I don't know who was the third one.
And I remember my father said, I don't speak Polish.
He spoke Russian.
He spoke, but, you know, he was all these years in the yeshivas.
So I said, no, if they'll see you, it'll be enough.
You don't have to speak.
And he went to-- to ask the authority not to do it.
Every town did by that time.
That was already in the '30s.
Because if I remember, maybe '35 or '36.
It-- it helped.
It helped.
They didn't-- I think her name was Prystorowa, the minister who brought that on the Jewish community.
And that's how it--
Well, when the war started.
It wasn't so bad.
The Russian came.
It's not that they-- they didn't kill no people.
But for us, my father was a-- a storekeeper.
We just made a living.
Because his brother-in-law didn't have a job, so he took him in, and he shared, with him, the profit, whatever it came in the store.
And they had five children.
But as being a storekeeper, he's already the [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the high-- the-- the-- that's-- And they were for the workers.
We weren't workers.
So in other cities, they used to send them to Siberia.
We weren't lucky, because, from Siberia, somebody came back.
They didn't do it in our town.
But we didn't-- couldn't participate in their parties or in their entertainments, because we were children from storekeepers.
That-- that I remember very well.
But they didn't kill people.
So that wasn't so bad.
Well, their-- their-- it's just that, we-- during the years, the storekeepers or they had factories, they exploit the workers.
And now it's all around, the workers now have the voice.
Yes, so they kept-- but in this-- you know.
It didn't--
The Russians came in.
They were with us two years.
And then when we heard that the-- the-- there's German coming, we hear that it's very bad.
They kill people.
But your mind couldn't absorb, how can somebody kill someone else for nothing?
You just couldn't think, how could it happen in that century?
You can take away, maybe, houses or something, but to kill people?
But when we saw some from Poland came to us, they ran away.
And they were-- they were telling.
And they-- some of them remained in our town.
Some of them went back to Russia, like-- So then we knew that something is bad.
But we stayed in our homes.
May I have a drink?
Of course.
[DRINKS]
We stayed in our houses.
And they came in, in 1941.
I think it was in-- in June.
I remember.
I mean, they didn't come to our town.
But we knew that they are somewhere around the big towns.
And eventually, they'll come to our town.
So in the morning, it was a big something is going on.
We opened the door.
There were-- there were, in the town, about five bad boys.
And if you give them the right, they will do anything.
And they probably went along with other people from the neighborhood, the town.
And they came.
And they opened the houses.
And they took whatever they wanted.
They tore the cushions.
All over were feathers, you know?
And we ran to the-- to the garden, hide ourselves.
In that day, they killed 11 people.
Between them was my uncle, Chonia Basewicz, with his oldest son, Milton-- Motle, like my brother's name.
And the 11 people, they were killed.
And they raped quite a few girls, wherever they-- they can find.
We had next-door neighbors, like in the back, Christian.
And they-- they said to us, you better go to our farm.
And we ran to their farm.
We stayed there, I think, a week.
And then they said it's a little bit quiet.
You can go back to your houses.
I know it was Tisha B'Av.
My father fast in the-- there, on the-- on the farm.
And the next day or the day after, we came to the-- to town.
I opened the door.
It was terrible to look about.
As long we are all alive, nothing matters.
I made something to eat.
And in the front, in the street, was his best friend from-- they grew up together and all this.
He says, you know what?
I'm going to go and see how my friend is.
And I said to him, I'm-- I'm going to go, with my sister, to the garden and pick up some vegetables.
And I'll make something for lunch.
As he went to his friend, and I just start to pick up the pickles.
And they were, for some reason, they grew so big, so-- And I hear some-- how do you call, 'pferd?' Horses.
Horses.
I went out from the garden, I see that everybody runs to the river.
The river was but two blocks from the garden.
With-- with my sister, we will-- we passed the river.
We went on the other side.
It's more safe.
We went into some houses, a house.
And they said, we're going to go to the town.
And we'll tell you what happened.
They came.
They said that they-- they took about 100-- over 100 people, just men.
And they took them to where the school, where the school is.
What-- what happened?
I don't-- we do not know.
Between them, was my father, with his friend, and the rabbi, the young rabbi, because the old rabbi died in 1938.
So he took his place.
And the best and the nicest from the city.
They kept them three days in the school.
And then we hear that-- what-- what will happen with them, I don't know.
My brother, I didn't know where he is.
But they didn't take, then, kids or women.
So I knew that he's somewhere.
And Friday morning, I remember, we-- we heard shots.
And I knew that's it.
But we don't-- didn't know, maybe they spared someone.
They took them to the cemetery.
They made two graves.
And they killed them all.
So we went.
We were already without parents.
So we went to our aunts, and we stayed with them.
How old were you then?
I was, then, probably 17.
Me and my sister, but when we came back to my aunt, my-- there he was.
When he said that the Germans sent them to pickup something, like for the horses, the hay.
And maybe, he wanted to send them away a few, they should run away.
Because maybe even from the German, there was somebody with a-- with a human touch.
But they didn't know that, if they will run away, for that reason, they'll-- they'll-- they'll kill somebody else.
Or whatever reason, I don't know.
So my brother ran out.
This time he says-- he says, no, no, you go to-- And we-- we have to go.
They send us, we have to go.
He didn't understand something else than that.
And so we came.
And we stayed with my aunt.
And then, in 1941, they made a ghetto.
He was killed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was killed, right away, in '41.
In '41, after the-- they killed the 11, a week, two after.
It could be in July.
After that, they made a ghetto.
So, in an our house, I didn't go.
There were three kids.
So they put there a few families.
We stayed with my aunts.
They-- they took three-- three streets.
And that was the ghetto.
The men, they used to take to work.
We weren't allowed to walk on the sidewalks.
The sidewalks were for people.
But we were not people.
So we were where the-- outside there, where the horses were going and whatever.
And they-- they had-- you had to put the-- the yellow thing, with the David's that-- Magin David.
And you lived in the ghetto.
So we had a few good neighbors, who used to bring, to the window, some food.
No.
No.
There wasn't there to work.
To work, it wasn't a town.
But what was there, the-- they appointed some local boys or a kommandant or some.
And they were-- they-- they did whatever they wanted to do.
And at night, they used to go and rape girls.
They used to go and do whatever they want.
But me and my sister, most of the time, we slept.
There was a-- a representative of the Jewish people.
His name was Shleimk Turkienicz.
I don't know if he was related to me, but he was-- he was a very nice man.
And at night, if they kept somebody in there, he used to put his own life and go and free them.
And he said, to me and my sister-- I was a very pretty girl-- you-- you come with-- you'll sleep in my place.
And I remember, he had a wife with kids.
On the floor, we slept most of the time to avoid that-- that harm.
But you see, when you are among-- all people were suffering, one way or the other.
First of all, there were already a lot of orphans.
Because if you're talking 100 fathers, the-- the town wasn't big, so you see orphans a lot.
So you are among them.
You are not singled out.
No.
No.
No, no.
No.
Polish were very little, because, even in town, there were just teachers Polish, policemen Polish.
There weren't living people Polish.
There was Russian people.
Polish people, as they occupied this land, they represented maybe the bigger cities, like Pinsk.
Or they-- they probably lived there.
But in our town, just the teachers and other people lived.
But mostly, the-- the-- the Russian Ukraine, it's closer.
The Ukrainian people lived there a lot.
I stayed a year.
I stayed a year in the ghetto.
And then there were rumors that this town, they killed everybody, this town.
Because from our part of the country, they didn't send to concentration camps.
For some reason, they didn't-- it didn't work for them.
So they just destroyed.
So when you were warned, they-- they were going to come to us.
But you hope the day wouldn't come.
But one day, the day came.
And the neighbors, the Christian neighbors came, and they said that-- that some Germans came.
And we-- we knew the Germans came.
And they made a big grave.
They said they may be a mile, a mile and a half.
And it's probably for you.
So where to run?
There was nowhere to run.
So we all gathered around Shleimk, but he couldn't save us.
And he said, I can't save you.
You better look your own way how to go out from here, if you want to go out, if there is a way to go out.
So, there I looked around.
My-- my brother is not here.
So my sister says to me, he probably is hiding in-- by our neighbor.
And they'll be the first one to be searched.
So it was dark.
I came through the back.
And I found him there.
And I said, you better come with us.
Whatever it'll happen, let's be together.
So he came.
And the other guy said, no, I'm not going to come.
And he stayed there.
So we went to one part of the-- and we start to run away.
He run over.
They start to shoot.
We were, again, on the other side.
We came back, I remember, to Shleimk.
And we hear the same-- the same story.
You don't wait here.
It's going to be morning, and then it's-- it's over.
And as I looked, and it was foggy.
The lake was foggy.
And I said to my sister, you know what?
We'll go.
As we were running out, there was a neighbor.
She was related to me.
She held on to my skirt.
She says, my father is not here or my brother.
I'll go with you.
I didn't know where I'm going.
And there was another girl.
She said, I-- I don't know where anybody is.
But she hold on to me.
And there was another mother, with two-- with two daughters.
One-- they were related to Weizmann, President Weizmann.
And-- and we all, we were five?
Four and three-- seven women, children.
We went-- we started to run.
Nobody shoots.
So we went to the other side.
And we came to a-- a Christian man, like a Ukrainian.
But he was good to us.
He says, look, I'd like to help you.
Can I stop you there for a moment?
Yeah.
